title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: polylinkR performs gene-based pathway enrichment analysis explicitly accounting linkage disequilibrium (LD) adjacent loci. Unlike standard pathway enrichment tools treat genes independent, polylinkR preserves innate linkage structure among genes across genome. reduces false positives arise genes pathway physically close share correlated signals. package implements novel circular permutation algorithm : 1. Links chromosomes single “circular” genome 2. Rotates circular genome create unique gene--score mappings 3. Preserves correlation structure among genes 4. Generates accurate null distributions pathway enrichment testing

title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: polylinkR requires following R packages: data.table - Fast data manipulation foreach - Parallel iteration support doFuture - Parallel backend future - Parallel processing framework dqrng - Fast random number generation Rfast - Fast statistical functions mgcv - Generalized additive models distances - Distance matrix calculations tdigest - Approximate quantile estimation cli - Command line interface formatting dependencies automatically installed install polylinkR CRAN GitHub.

title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: polylinkR requires three core input files: objID: Unique gene identifier objStat: Gene-level statistic (e.g., selection score, p-value) chr, startpos, endpos: Genomic coordinates (optional recommended) setID: Unique pathway/set identifier Additional optional metadata columns setID: Set identifier (must match set.info) objID: Gene identifier (must match obj.info) Optional files include: - var.info - SNP-level statistics computing gene scores - rec.rate - Recombination rates converting physical genetic distances

title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: vignette uses Anatolia_EF_CLR dataset included polylinkR. dataset contains gene-level statistics population genetics study Anatolia population, selection signals computed using Eigenstrat format data. Let’s examine structure included data: Anatolia_EF_CLR object contains: - objID: Gene identifiers - objStat: Gene selection statistics (higher values indicate stronger signals) - objName: Gene symbols (e.g., A1BG, A2M) - chr: Chromosome numbers - startpos/endpos: Genomic positions base pairs - GeneLength: Gene length base pairs Let’s also examine gene sets:
code:
# Load the example datasets data("Anatolia_EF_CLR") data("PolyLinkR_SetInfo") data("PolyLinkR_SetObj") data("rr.dt")  # Examine the gene information head(Anatolia_EF_CLR) str(Anatolia_EF_CLR)  # Check the number of genes cat("Number of genes:", nrow(Anatolia_EF_CLR), "
") cat("Number of chromosomes:", length(unique(Anatolia_EF_CLR$chr)), "
") # Gene set information head(PolyLinkR_SetInfo) cat("Number of gene sets:", nrow(PolyLinkR_SetInfo), "

")  # Check how many genes are in each set (sample) set_counts <- table(PolyLinkR_SetObj$setID) summary(as.vector(set_counts))

title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: plR_read() function entry point polylinkR analysis. validates input files, performs quality checks, creates plR class object subsequent functions operate .

previous_headings: Basic Analysis Walkthrough > Step 1: Reading Data with plR_read()
title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: Key parameters reading data: input.path: Directory containing input files (alternative: specify individual file paths) obj.info.path, set.info.path, set.obj.path: Individual file paths rec.rate.path: Optional recombination rate file coordinate conversion min.set.n: Minimum gene set size (default: 2, must ≥ 2) max.set.n: Maximum gene set size (default: Inf) set.merge: Threshold merging similar gene sets (default: 0.95 = 95% similarity) rem.genes: Whether remove genes identical positions (default: FALSE) Since data loaded R already, let’s save temporary files demonstrate reading process: Now read data polylinkR: plR_read() function returns plR S3 object containing three data.tables: - obj.info: Gene information validated scores - set.info: Gene set information sizes - set.obj: Gene--set mappings
code: # Create temporary directory for demonstration temp_dir <- tempdir()  # Write data to files data.table::fwrite(as.data.table(Anatolia_EF_CLR),                     file.path(temp_dir, "obj.info.tsv"), sep = "\t") data.table::fwrite(as.data.table(PolyLinkR_SetInfo),                     file.path(temp_dir, "set.info.tsv"), sep = "\t") data.table::fwrite(as.data.table(PolyLinkR_SetObj),                     file.path(temp_dir, "set.obj.tsv"), sep = "\t") data.table::fwrite(as.data.table(rr.dt),                     file.path(temp_dir, "rec.rate.tsv"), sep = "\t")  # List the files cat("Files prepared in:", temp_dir, "\n") list.files(temp_dir, pattern = "\\.tsv$") # Read the prepared data plr_obj <- plR_read(   input.path = temp_dir,   min.set.n = 5,      # Only include gene sets with 5+ genes   set.merge = 0.95,   # Merge gene sets with ≥95% overlap   verbose = TRUE )  # Check the result class(plr_obj) print(plr_obj)

title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: plR_permute() function performs gene set enrichment testing using permutation procedure : 1. Creates null distributions permuting gene scores preserving linkage structure 2. Fits Generalized Pareto Distributions (GPD) tails accurate small p-values 3. Computes enrichment p-values gene set

previous_headings: Basic Analysis Walkthrough > Step 2: Permutation Testing with plR_permute()
title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: Key parameters permutation: n.perm: Number permutations (default: 500,000). permutations = accurate p-values slower. Minimum: 50,000. n.boot: Number bootstrap replicates GPD fitting (default: 30). Higher = stable slower. alt: Test direction - "upper" enrichment (default) "lower" depletion gpd.cutoff: Tail probability threshold GPD fitting (default: 500/n.perm) seed: Random seed reproducibility n.cores: Number cores parallel processing demonstration, ’ll use smaller number permutations speed: plR_permute() function adds new columns set.info: - setScore.std: Standardized gene set scores - setScore.std.p: P-values enrichment Let’s examine significant results:
code: # Run permutation analysis with reduced permutations for vignette speed plr_perm <- plR_permute(   plR.input = plr_obj,   n.perm = 50000,      # Use 50k for demonstration (use 500k+ for real analysis)   n.boot = 10,         # Reduced bootstrap replicates   seed = 42,           # For reproducibility   n.cores = 1,         # Single core for vignette   verbose = TRUE )  # Check the results print(plr_perm) # Extract set info with p-values set_results <- plr_perm$set.info  # Show top significant sets head(set_results[order(setScore.std.p)][, .(setID, setN, setScore.std, setScore.std.p)])  # Summary of p-values summary(set_results$setScore.std.p)

what: Step 3: Rescaling for Autocorrelation with plR_rescale()
title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: plR_rescale() function adjusts gene set scores account genetic autocorrelation - tendency nearby genes correlated statistics due LD. step crucial accurate p-value estimation.

previous_headings: Basic Analysis Walkthrough > Step 3: Rescaling for Autocorrelation with plR_rescale()
title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: Key parameters rescaling: rescale: Whether perform rescaling (default: TRUE). Set FALSE estimate autocorrelation. fast: Use fast analytical rescaling (default: TRUE) vs. slower empirical rescaling cgm.range: Maximum inter-gene distance autocovariance estimation (default: 2 Mbp 2 cM) cgm.bin: Minimum gene pairs per lag bin (default: 30) min.rho: Minimum correlation threshold (default: 1e-5) plR_rescale() function adds: - setScore.rs: Rescaled gene set scores (accounting autocorrelation) - setScore.rs.p: P-values rescaled scores Compare standard vs. rescaled scores:
code: # Rescale for genetic autocorrelation plr_rescale <- plR_rescale(   plR.input = plr_perm,   rescale = TRUE,   fast = TRUE,         # Use fast analytical method   verbose = TRUE )  # Check results print(plr_rescale) # Compare standard and rescaled results comparison <- plr_rescale$set.info[, .(setID, setN,                                         setScore.std, setScore.std.p,                                        setScore.rs, setScore.rs.p)] head(comparison[order(setScore.rs.p)])

what: Step 4: Pruning and FDR Correction with plR_prune()
title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: plR_prune() function performs multiple testing correction accounting shared genes pathways. Gene sets often overlap, step ensures FDR control accounts dependencies.

previous_headings: Basic Analysis Walkthrough > Step 4: Pruning and FDR Correction with plR_prune()
title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: Key parameters pruning: n.fdr: Number permuted datasets FDR estimation (default: 300). Must divisible 100. est.pi0: Whether estimate proportion true nulls (default: TRUE) tolerance: Convergence tolerance pi0 estimation (default: 1e-3)
code: # Note: This step is computationally intensive and skipped in vignette # Run it for your actual analyses  plr_final <- plR_prune(   plR.input = plr_rescale,   n.fdr = 300,         # Number of FDR replicates   est.pi0 = TRUE,      # Estimate proportion of true nulls   verbose = TRUE )  # Results include: # - setScore.pr: Pruned gene set scores # - setScore.pr.p: P-values for pruned scores   # - setScore.pr.q: FDR-corrected q-values

title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: common way visualize pathway enrichment results Manhattan-style plot showing -log10(p-values) across gene sets:
code: # Prepare data for plotting plot_data <- plr_rescale$set.info[, .(setID, setN, setScore.std.p, setScore.rs.p)] plot_data[, log_p_std := -log10(setScore.std.p)] plot_data[, log_p_rs := -log10(setScore.rs.p)]  # Create significance threshold line threshold <- -log10(0.05 / nrow(plot_data))  # Bonferroni  # Plot p <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = seq_along(setID), y = log_p_rs)) +   geom_point(aes(color = log_p_rs > 5), alpha = 0.6, size = 2) +   geom_hline(yintercept = threshold, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +   geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dotted", color = "blue") +   scale_color_manual(values = c("grey50", "red"),                       labels = c("Not significant", "Suggestive")) +   labs(     title = "Gene Set Enrichment Results",     subtitle = "polylinkR Analysis of Anatolia_EF_CLR Dataset",     x = "Gene Set Index",     y = expression(-log[10](p-value)),     color = "Significance"   ) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(     legend.position = "bottom",     plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")   )  print(p)

what: Volcano Plot: Effect Size vs. P-value
title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: Another useful visualization volcano plot showing relationship effect size (gene set score) statistical significance:
code: # Prepare volcano plot data volcano_data <- plr_rescale$set.info[, .(setID, setN,                                            setScore.rs, setScore.rs.p,                                           setScore.std)] volcano_data[, log_p := -log10(setScore.rs.p)] volcano_data[, significance := ifelse(setScore.rs.p < 0.05 / nrow(volcano_data),                                      "Significant",                                      ifelse(setScore.rs.p < 0.05,                                             "Suggestive", "Not significant"))]  # Plot p2 <- ggplot(volcano_data, aes(x = setScore.rs, y = log_p)) +   geom_point(aes(color = significance, size = setN), alpha = 0.6) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("Not significant" = "grey50",                                 "Suggestive" = "orange",                                "Significant" = "red")) +   scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 4)) +   geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dotted") +   labs(     title = "Volcano Plot: Gene Set Enrichment",     x = "Rescaled Gene Set Score",     y = expression(-log[10](p-value)),     color = "Significance",     size = "Gene Set Size"   ) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "right")  print(p2)

title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: Start smaller n.perm: initial exploration, use n.perm = 50,000. publication-quality results, use 500,000+. Check gene set sizes: Gene sets fewer 5 genes may yield unreliable results. Use min.set.n filter. Monitor memory usage: Large datasets many gene sets can require substantial memory. Consider using fewer cores memory limited. Use appropriate coordinates: data uses physical positions (bp), provide recombination rates accurate distance calculations. Save intermediate results: pipeline can run incrementally, save objects step troubleshooting.

title: Basic Workflow with polylinkR
text: Mismatched IDs: Ensure objID obj.info matches objID set.obj, setID set.info matches setID set.obj. Missing coordinates: Genomic coordinates optional plR_read() required plR_rescale(). missing, rescaling fail. Duplicate entries: functions handle duplicates automatically, ’s best clean data beforehand. Insufficient permutations: permutations lead unstable p-values. minimum recommended 50,000. Forgetting seed: Always set seed reproducible results, especially parallel processing.

what: 1. Why Control for Confounders in Polygenic Linkage Analysis
title: Controlling for Confounding Variables in Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: Confounding variables factors influence genetic architecture trait studied, potentially leading spurious associations. polygenic pathway enrichment analysis, common confounders include: Age: Gene expression methylation patterns change age Sex: X-chromosome inactivation sex-specific genetic effects Ancestry: Population stratification can create LD patterns unrelated trait biology Without accounting factors, pathway enrichment tests may: Inflate false positive rates: Genes correlated confounders appear enriched Mask true signals: Real pathway associations may obscured confounder-induced noise Produce irreproducible results: Confounder-driven associations rarely replicate polylinkR addresses local quadratic regression approach : Calculates Mahalanobis distances genes covariate space Applies kernel weighting identify genetically proximal genes similar covariate profiles Regresses confounder effects preserving biological signal

what: 2. Creating Covariate Dataframes with Sample IDs Matching Genetic Data
title: Controlling for Confounding Variables in Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: Anatolia_EF_CLR dataset contains gene-level statistics covariate columns. vignette, ’ll create synthetic confounders simulate real-world scenarios. First, let’s examine structure data: Key columns covariate integration: objID: Unique gene identifier (must match obj.info covariates) objStat: Gene-level test statistic (score deconfounded) chr, startpos, endpos: Genomic coordinates (required deconfounding) Now, let’s create synthetic covariates simulate age, sex, ancestry effects: Now let’s simulate covariates might confound gene scores:
code: # Load the example data data(Anatolia_EF_CLR)  # Check the structure str(Anatolia_EF_CLR) head(Anatolia_EF_CLR) # Create a copy of the data for modification obj_info <- copy(Anatolia_EF_CLR)  # Set seed for reproducibility set.seed(42) n_genes <- nrow(obj_info)  # Cov1: Age-related methylation proxy # Genes on certain chromosomes tend to have age-related expression changes obj_info[, Cov1 := rnorm(.N, mean = 0, sd = 1)] # Add chromosome-specific patterns (simulating age effects) obj_info[chr %in% c(1, 3, 5), Cov1 := Cov1 + 0.5] obj_info[chr %in% c(2, 4, 6), Cov1 := Cov1 - 0.3]  # Cov2: Sex chromosome dosage # X chromosome genes have different expression patterns obj_info[, Cov2 := rnorm(.N, mean = 0, sd = 1)] obj_info[chr == 23, Cov2 := Cov2 + 1.5]  # X chromosome enrichment obj_info[chr == 24, Cov2 := Cov2 - 1.0]  # Y chromosome depletion  # Cov3: Ancestry principal component proxy # Spatial autocorrelation simulating population structure obj_info[, gene_mid := (startpos + endpos) / 2] obj_info[, Cov3 := rnorm(.N, mean = 0, sd = 1)] # Create regional patterns for (chrom in unique(obj_info$chr)) {   chr_genes <- which(obj_info$chr == chrom)   if (length(chr_genes) > 10) {     # Smooth spatial pattern     positions <- obj_info$gene_mid[chr_genes]     sorted_idx <- order(positions)     trend <- sin(positions[sorted_idx] / max(positions) * 2 * pi)     obj_info$Cov3[chr_genes[sorted_idx]] <- obj_info$Cov3[chr_genes[sorted_idx]] + trend   } }  # Remove temporary column obj_info[, gene_mid := NULL]  # Display the new covariate columns head(obj_info[, c("objID", "objName", "Cov1", "Cov2", "Cov3")])  # Summary statistics summary(obj_info[, .(Cov1, Cov2, Cov3)]) # Add confounding effect to gene scores # In real data, these effects are already present in the objStat values # Here we demonstrate by adding synthetic confounding  obj_info[, objStat_original := objStat]  # Add covariate effects to simulate confounded data obj_info[, objStat := objStat +             0.3 * Cov1 +    # Age effect            0.2 * Cov2 +    # Sex effect              0.25 * Cov3]    # Ancestry effect  # Check correlation between covariates and scores print("Correlations with confounded scores:") cor_matrix <- cor(obj_info[, .(objStat, Cov1, Cov2, Cov3)]) print(cor_matrix)

what: 3. Using the covar Parameter in plR_permute() and plR_rescale()
title: Controlling for Confounding Variables in Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: polylinkR, covariates detected automatically columns Cov prefix obj.info file. deconfounding process happens plR_permute() key parameters: md.meth: Mahalanobis distance method ("robust" rank-based, "raw" Pearson) kern.bound: Genomic distance flanking region exclude kernel weights kern.func: Kernel function ("normal", "exponential", "inverse") kern.scale: Scaling parameter kernel decay kern.wt.max: Maximum weight single gene Let’s prepare polylinkR input covariate-enriched obj.info:
code: # Load additional required data data(PolyLinkR_SetInfo) data(PolyLinkR_SetObj)  # Create temporary directory for input files temp_dir <- tempfile("polylinkR_covar_demo") dir.create(temp_dir, recursive = TRUE)  # Write files with covariate columns fwrite(obj_info, file.path(temp_dir, "ObjInfo.txt"), sep = "\t") fwrite(PolyLinkR_SetInfo, file.path(temp_dir, "SetInfo.txt"), sep = "\t") fwrite(PolyLinkR_SetObj, file.path(temp_dir, "SetObj.txt"), sep = "\t")  # Read the data into polylinkR plr_obj <- plR_read(   input.path = temp_dir,   verbose = TRUE )  # Verify the object structure print(plr_obj)

title: Controlling for Confounding Variables in Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: vignette, ’ll demonstrate setup:
code: # Run deconfounding and permutation with covariate control # Note: Using reduced permutations for demonstration (normally use 1e5 or more)  plr_with_covar <- plR_permute(   plR.input = plr_obj,   permute = TRUE,   n.perm = 1e4,  # Reduced for demo; use 1e5+ for real analysis   n.boot = 10,   # Reduced for demo   md.meth = "robust",      # Use rank-based robust method   kern.bound = 1e5,        # 100kb flanking region   kern.func = "normal",    # Gaussian kernel   kern.scale = 2,          # Standard decay   kern.wt.max = 0.05,      # Max 5% weight for any gene   seed = 42,   verbose = TRUE,   n.cores = 1 ) # Check that covariates are properly detected obj_info_check <- copy(plr_obj$obj.info)  # Identify covariate columns cov_names <- grep("^Cov[0-9]+$", names(obj_info_check), value = TRUE) cat("Detected covariate columns:", paste(cov_names, collapse = ", "), "\n") cat("Number of covariates:", length(cov_names), "\n")

text: Essential covariates: - Age (continuous binned) - Sex (binary factor) - Ancestry principal components (typically PC1-PC5) - Technical factors (batch, platform, sequencing depth) Optional covariates: - Cell type proportions (bulk tissue) - BMI, smoking status (relevant trait) - Sample collection time/season

title: Controlling for Confounding Variables in Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: trait interest : Never include outcome variable covariate Mediators: Variables causal pathway genes trait Colliders: Variables influenced genes trait many PCs: Including >10 ancestry PCs often captures noise rather structure

title: Controlling for Confounding Variables in Polygenic Linkage Analysis
code: # Example preprocessing pipeline preprocess_covariates <- function(cov_data) {      # 1. Handle missing values   cov_data <- na.omit(cov_data)      # 2. Standardize continuous covariates   cont_cols <- c("Age", "BMI", "PC1", "PC2", "PC3")   for (col in cont_cols) {     if (col %in% names(cov_data)) {       cov_data[[col]] <- scale(cov_data[[col]])[, 1]     }   }      # 3. Encode categorical variables   if ("Sex" %in% names(cov_data)) {     cov_data$Sex <- as.numeric(factor(cov_data$Sex)) - 1  # 0/1 encoding   }      # 4. Remove collinear covariates   cor_matrix <- cor(cov_data[, -1])  # Exclude ID column   high_cor <- which(abs(cor_matrix) > 0.9 & upper.tri(cor_matrix), arr.ind = TRUE)   if (nrow(high_cor) > 0) {     cat("Highly correlated covariate pairs detected:\n")     for (i in seq_len(nrow(high_cor))) {       cat(names(cov_data)[high_cor[i, 1] + 1], "~",            names(cov_data)[high_cor[i, 2] + 1], "\n")     }   }      return(cov_data) }

previous_headings: 3. Function Parameters Reference Tables > 3.1 plR_permute() Parameters
title: Input Formats and Parameters Reference
text: n.perm - Higher values increase precision small p-values increase computation time. Default 500,000 provides good precision ~1e-5. n.boot - bootstrap replicates improve estimation null distribution quantiles. alt - Use "upper" testing enrichment genes high scores (e.g., positive selection), "lower" low scores (e.g., purifying selection). md.meth - "robust" converts covariates ranks computing Mahalanobis distances (recommended non-normal covariates). "raw" uses original values. kern.bound - Defines buffer region around gene overlapping genes receive reduced weights. "auto" sets 0.1 Mbp 0.1 cM. kern.func - Determines distance--weight conversion occurs: - "normal": Gaussian decay - "exponential": Exponential decay - "inverse": Inverse distance weighting

title: Input Formats and Parameters Reference
text: Rescale gene set scores genetic autocorrelation.

previous_headings: 3. Function Parameters Reference Tables > 3.2 plR_rescale() Parameters
title: Input Formats and Parameters Reference
text: fast - TRUE, uses analytical rescaling (much faster). FALSE, performs full empirical rescaling (accurate computationally intensive). cgm.range - Defines maximum distance computing empirical autocovariance. "auto" sets 3 Mbp 3 cM. cgm.bin - Controls binning empirical covariances. Larger values create smoother correlograms may miss local structure. emp.bayes - Framework regularizing chromosome-level parameters: - "full": Correlated random effects (requires ≥15 chromosomes) - "reduced": Independent random effects - "auto": Chooses based chromosome count min.rho - Correlations threshold set zero, reducing noise covariance matrix.

FALSE, assumes pi0 = 1 (conservative). tolerance - Controls pi0 estimation stops. Smaller values require iterations convergence.

what: Introduction to Parallel Processing in polylinkR
title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: polylinkR performs gene-based pathway enrichment analysis using permutation approach accounts linkage disequilibrium (LD) structure. permutation step—generates thousands bootstrap iterations create null distributions—embarrassingly parallel. means permutation iteration independent can computed simultaneously multiple CPU cores.

previous_headings: Introduction to Parallel Processing in polylinkR
title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: bootstrap iterations plR_permute() involve: Randomly sampling genes preserving LD structure Computing pathway scores sample Accumulating statistics across iterations operations independent across iterations, making ideal candidates parallelization. performance gains can substantial: Note: Benchmarks approximate vary hardware.

previous_headings: Introduction to Parallel Processing in polylinkR
title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: plR_permute(): primary beneficiary—bootstrap iterations distributed across cores Large gene sets: genes = computation per iteration = greater parallel efficiency High iteration counts: 10,000+ permutations show best speedup Operations don’t benefit parallelization: - File /O plR_read() - Single-threaded GPD fitting - Memory-bound rescaling operations

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: polylinkR uses future framework parallel processing, provides unified interface across different parallel backends. recommended approach uses doFuture foreach.

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
code: # Install required packages (run once) install.packages(c("foreach", "doFuture", "progressr"))

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: standard workflow involves: Plan future: Select parallel backend Register foreach: Enable %dopar% operations Run parallel code: Execute polylinkR functions Clean : Reset sequential mode
code: # Load required libraries library(foreach) library(doFuture) library(progressr)  # Check available cores ncores <- parallel::detectCores() cat("Available cores:", ncores, "\n")  # Plan parallel execution with multisession (works on all platforms) # Best practice: leave 1 core for the OS workers <- max(1, ncores - 1) cat("Using workers:", workers, "\n")

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: Windows: - Use plan(multisession) process-based parallelism - Avoid multicore (forking) ’s unstable Windows Linux/Mac: - plan(multicore) offers lower overhead via forking - plan(multisession) also works stable
code: # Windows (recommended) plan(multisession, workers = workers)  # Linux/Mac (lower overhead option) plan(multicore, workers = workers)

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: Key guideline: Always leave least 1 core free operating system processes. prevents system lag memory swapping.

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: Let’s compare sequential parallel execution using small example:
code: # Load example data data_path <- system.file("extdata", "tiny_polylinkR", package = "polylinkR") plr_obj <- plR_read(data_path, verbose = FALSE)  # Sequential execution (default) cat("Running sequential...\n") t1 <- system.time({   result_seq <- plR_permute(     plr_obj,     N.SNPs = 100,  # Small number for quick demo     N.iter = 100,     verbose = FALSE   ) }) cat("Sequential time:", t1["elapsed"], "seconds\n")

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: enable parallel processing polylinkR, need : Plan future backend Register foreach Call polylinkR functions (’ll use %dopar% internally)
code:
# Set up parallel backend plan(multisession, workers = 2)  # Use 2 workers for vignette doFuture::registerDoFuture()  # Parallel execution cat("Running parallel...
") t2 <- system.time({   handlers(global = TRUE)   result_par <- plR_permute(     plr_obj,     N.SNPs = 100,     N.iter = 100,     verbose = FALSE   ) }) cat("Parallel time:", t2["elapsed"], "seconds
")  # Calculate speedup speedup <- t1["elapsed"] / t2["elapsed"] cat("Speedup:", round(speedup, 2), "×
")  # Reset to sequential plan(sequential)

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: running long parallel jobs, progress bars help monitor execution: silent/background execution:
code: # Enable global progress handlers handlers(global = TRUE) handlers("txtprogressbar")  # Text-based progress bar  # Run with progress reporting result <- plR_permute(   plr_obj,   N.SNPs = 1000,   N.iter = 10000,   verbose = TRUE  # Enables progress output ) # Disable progress for batch jobs handlers(global = FALSE)  result <- plR_permute(   plr_obj,   N.SNPs = 1000,   N.iter = 10000,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: ’s template benchmarking specific hardware:
code: benchmark_parallel <- function(plr_obj, iterations = c(100, 1000, 5000)) {   results <- data.frame(     iterations = integer(),     cores = integer(),     time_seconds = numeric()   )      # Test different core counts   core_options <- c(1, 2, 4, parallel::detectCores() - 1)      for (n_iter in iterations) {     for (n_cores in core_options) {       if (n_cores == 1) {         plan(sequential)       } else {         plan(multisession, workers = n_cores)         doFuture::registerDoFuture()       }              t <- system.time({         res <- plR_permute(plr_obj, N.SNPs = 500, N.iter = n_iter, verbose = FALSE)       })              results <- rbind(results, data.frame(         iterations = n_iter,         cores = n_cores,         time_seconds = t["elapsed"]       ))              # Clean up workers       if (n_cores > 1) {         plan(sequential)       }     }   }      return(results) }  # Run benchmark bench_results <- benchmark_parallel(plr_obj) print(bench_results)

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: Based typical workstation hardware (Intel/AMD 8-core CPU): Efficiency = (Speedup / Core Count) × 100. Higher iteration counts show better efficiency due reduced overhead amortization.

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: Parallel execution increases memory usage: Sequential: Memory × 1 N workers: Memory × (1 + N × 0.3) approximately worker maintains copy necessary data structures. large datasets:
code: # Check available memory mem_info <- gc(reset = TRUE) cat("Current memory usage:", mem_info[2, 2], "MB\n")  # Estimate parallel memory estimate_memory <- function(n_workers, base_mb = 500) {   # Base usage + overhead per worker   total <- base_mb * (1 + n_workers * 0.3)   return(total) }  available_workers <- function(total_ram_gb, base_mb = 500) {   max_workers <- floor((total_ram_gb * 1024 - base_mb) / (base_mb * 0.3))   return(min(max_workers, parallel::detectCores() - 1)) }  cat("Recommended workers for 16GB RAM:", available_workers(16), "\n")

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: Skip parallel processing : Overhead exceeds computation time Sequential may faster Communication overhead dominates Swapping kills performance Process creation overhead can significant Rule thumb: Parallelize expected time > 30 seconds iterations > 5,000.

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: extremely large iteration counts, use chunking reduce scheduling overhead: polylinkR automatically handles chunking internally large N.iter values.
code: # Manual chunking for fine control plan(multisession, workers = 4) doFuture::registerDoFuture()  # Divide work into chunks N.iter <- 50000 n_chunks <- 20 chunk_size <- N.iter / n_chunks  # Process chunks in parallel results <- foreach(i = 1:n_chunks, .combine = c) %dopar% {   # Each worker processes a chunk   process_chunk(chunk_size) }

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: hitting memory limits:
code: # Strategy 1: Reduce workers plan(multisession, workers = 2)  # Fewer workers = less memory  # Strategy 2: Process in batches batch_permute <- function(plr_obj, n_iter_total, batch_size = 1000) {   n_batches <- ceiling(n_iter_total / batch_size)   all_results <- list()      for (batch in 1:n_batches) {     # Process one batch at a time     res <- plR_permute(plr_obj, N.iter = batch_size, verbose = FALSE)     all_results[[batch]] <- res     gc()  # Force garbage collection between batches   }      # Combine results   return(combine_results(all_results)) }  # Strategy 3: Use disk-backed storage for large objects options(future.globals.maxSize = 1024 * 1024^2)  # 1GB limit

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: Always clean parallel resources prevent zombie processes memory leaks: thorough cleanup (especially errors):
code: # Standard cleanup - returns to sequential processing plan(sequential)  # Verify cleanup cat("Current plan:", class(plan()), "\n") # Reset all future state future::resetWorkers(plan()) plan(sequential)  # Force garbage collection gc()  # On Windows, check for orphaned R processes in Task Manager

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: Windows: - Always use multisession (never multicore) - Limit workers 4-8 stability - Watch antivirus interference socket connections Linux: - multicore offers best performance - Check ulimit settings process limits - Consider cgroups resource control shared systems macOS: - multicore works well Intel Macs - Apple Silicon: multisession reliable - Check “System Settings > General > Login Items” process limits

title: Parallel Processing for Performance
text: ’s complete, production-ready script parallel pathway analysis:
code:
#!/usr/bin/env Rscript # parallel_pathway_analysis.R  library(polylinkR) library(foreach) library(doFuture) library(progressr)  # Configuration CONFIG <- list(   data_path = "path/to/your/data",   output_path = "results.rds",   n_iterations = 10000,   n_snps = 1000,   verbose = TRUE )  # Setup parallel backend setup_parallel <- function() {   ncores <- parallel::detectCores()   workers <- max(1, ncores - 1)      cat("Setting up parallel backend with", workers, "workers
")      # Platform-specific setup   if (.Platform$OS.type == "windows") {     plan(multisession, workers = workers)   } else {     # Try multicore first, fall back to multisession     tryCatch({       plan(multicore, workers = workers)     }, error = function(e) {       message("multicore failed, using multisession: ", e$message)       plan(multisession, workers = workers)     })   }      doFuture::registerDoFuture()   handlers(global = TRUE)      return(workers) }  # Main analysis main <- function() {   # Setup   workers <- setup_parallel()   on.exit({     cat("Cleaning up parallel resources...
")     plan(sequential)   })  # Ensure cleanup even on error      # Read data   cat("Reading input data...
")   plr_obj <- plR_read(CONFIG$data_path, verbose = CONFIG$verbose)      # Run parallel permutation   cat("Running parallel permutation analysis...
")   start_time <- Sys.time()      result <- plR_permute(     plr_obj,     N.SNPs = CONFIG$n_snps,     N.iter = CONFIG$n_iterations,     verbose = CONFIG$verbose   )      end_time <- Sys.time()   elapsed <- as.numeric(end_time - start_time, units = "secs")      cat("Analysis complete!
")   cat("Total time:", round(elapsed, 2), "seconds
")   cat("Effective rate:", round(CONFIG$n_iterations / elapsed, 1), "iterations/sec
")      # Save results   saveRDS(result, CONFIG$output_path)   cat("Results saved to:", CONFIG$output_path, "
")      # Cleanup (also handled by on.exit)   plan(sequential)      return(result) }  # Run if executed as script if (!interactive()) {   result <- main() }

code: safe_parallel <- function(expr) {   # Setup   plan(multisession, workers = 4)   doFuture::registerDoFuture()      # Execute with error handling and cleanup   tryCatch({     result <- expr     return(result)   }, finally = {     # Always execute cleanup     cat("Shutting down parallel workers...\n")     plan(sequential)     gc()   }) }  # Usage result <- safe_parallel({   plR_permute(plr_obj, N.SNPs = 1000, N.iter = 10000) })

title: Getting Started with polylinkR
text: polylinkR R package gene-based pathway enrichment accounts linkage disequilibrium among loci. vignette demonstrates basic workflow using included tiny_polylinkR example dataset.

title: Getting Started with polylinkR
text: polylinkR workflow follows four sequential steps: plR_read() - Read validate input files plR_permute() - Generate permutation nulls compute p-values plR_rescale() - Rescale genetic autocorrelation plR_prune() - Prune gene sets compute FDR

title: Getting Started with polylinkR

title: Getting Started with polylinkR
text: polylinkR requires three input files:

title: Getting Started with polylinkR
text: Contains gene-level information: objID: Unique gene identifier objStat: Gene-level statistic (e.g., p-value, z-score) Optional columns: chr, startpos, endpos (coordinate-based analysis)

title: Getting Started with polylinkR
text: Contains gene set definitions: setID: Unique set identifier setName: Human-readable set name setSource: Source database (optional)

title: Getting Started with polylinkR
text: Contains gene--set relationships: setID: Set identifier (must match SetInfo.txt) objID: Gene identifier (must match ObjInfo.txt)

title: Getting Started with polylinkR
text: package active development. Results independently validated use production clinical decisions.

title: Getting Started with polylinkR
text: core statistical algorithms (permutation nulls, GPD fitting, rescaling, pruning) frozen modified without explicit maintainer approval.

title: Getting Started with polylinkR
text: reading data, typically proceed pipeline: plR_permute() - Generate permutation-based null distributions plR_rescale() - Account genetic autocorrelation plR_prune() - Identify significant gene sets See function documentation (?plR_permute, ?plR_rescale, ?plR_prune) details subsequent steps.

what: 1. What Recombination Rates Add to the Analysis
title: Incorporating Recombination Rates into Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: Recombination rates fundamental understanding genetic linkage linkage disequilibrium (LD) patterns. Incorporating polygenic pathway enrichment analysis provides several advantages:

previous_headings: 1. What Recombination Rates Add to the Analysis
title: Incorporating Recombination Rates into Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: Recombination shapes LD patterns: - Hotspots recombination break LD blocks - Cold regions maintain long-range LD - relationship physical distance (bp) genetic distance (cM) varies across genome physical distance alone insufficient: - 1 Mb recombination hotspot ≠ 1 Mb cold region (genetically) - Genes 500kb apart hotspot may less correlated genes 2Mb apart cold region - Accounting recombination improves estimates genetic autocorrelation

previous_headings: 1. What Recombination Rates Add to the Analysis
title: Incorporating Recombination Rates into Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: Autocorrelation estimation: Distance genes measured centiMorgans (cM) instead base pairs Gene set rescaling: Decorrelation weights based genetic rather physical distance Crossover-aware null models: Permutation nulls respect recombination boundaries

what: 2. Loading Recombination Map Data (HapMap Format, Averaged from deCODE)

previous_headings: 2. Loading Recombination Map Data (HapMap Format, Averaged from deCODE)
title: Incorporating Recombination Rates into Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: cM column provides cumulative genetic map position. can calculate genetic distances positions:
code:
# Example: Calculate genetic distance between two positions on chromosome 1 rr_chr1 <- human_sexavg_RR_Bherer2017[chr == 1]  # Find genetic positions for two physical positions pos1 <- 1000000 pos2 <- 5000000  # Interpolate to get genetic distances cM1 <- approx(rr_chr1$pos, rr_chr1$cM, xout = pos1)$y cM2 <- approx(rr_chr1$pos, rr_chr1$cM, xout = pos2)$y  if (!is.na(cM1) && !is.na(cM2)) {   genetic_distance <- abs(cM2 - cM1)   physical_distance <- abs(pos2 - pos1) / 1e6  # in Mb      cat("Physical distance:", physical_distance, "Mb
")   cat("Genetic distance:", round(genetic_distance, 4), "cM
")   cat("Recombination rate in this region:",        round(genetic_distance / physical_distance, 4), "cM/Mb
") }

code: # Load example gene data data(Anatolia_EF_CLR)  # Calculate midpoints gene_data <- copy(Anatolia_EF_CLR) gene_data[, midpos := (startpos + endpos) / 2]  # Show first few genes with their coordinates head(gene_data[, .(objID, objName, chr, startpos, endpos, midpos)])

previous_headings: 3. Matching SNP Positions to Recombination Rates
title: Incorporating Recombination Rates into Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: plR_read() function handles conversion automatically rec.rate file provided. ’s interpolation works internally:
code: # Function to interpolate genetic positions interpolate_cM <- function(chr_num, positions, rr_data) {   rr_chr <- rr_data[chr == chr_num]   approx(rr_chr$pos, rr_chr$cM, xout = positions, rule = 2)$y }  # Example: Get genetic positions for first 10 genes genes_sample <- gene_data[chr %in% 1:22][1:10]  genes_sample[, genetic_pos := interpolate_cM(chr, midpos, human_sexavg_RR_Bherer2017)]  cat("Example gene positions:\n") print(genes_sample[, .(objName, chr, midpos, genetic_pos)])

previous_headings: 4. Using the rr Parameter in Functions
title: Incorporating Recombination Rates into Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: map.fun parameter controls recombination rates converted genetic distances: r recombination fraction.
code: # Example: Compare mapping functions recomb_fraction <- seq(0, 0.5, length.out = 100)  # Haldane mapping haldane <- function(r) -0.5 * log(1 - 2 * r)  # Kosambi mapping kosambi <- function(r) 0.25 * log((1 + 2 * r) / (1 - 2 * r))  # Plot comparison (excluding r = 0.5 where functions are undefined) r_valid <- recomb_fraction[recomb_fraction < 0.49]  plot(r_valid, kosambi(r_valid), type = "l", col = "blue", lwd = 2,      xlab = "Recombination Fraction (r)", ylab = "Genetic Distance (cM)",      main = "Comparison of Mapping Functions") lines(r_valid, haldane(r_valid), col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2) legend("topleft", legend = c("Kosambi (default)", "Haldane"),        col = c("blue", "red"), lwd = 2, lty = c(1, 2))

what: 5. Visualizing Linkage Signals Relative to Recombination Hotspots
title: Incorporating Recombination Rates into Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: Understanding signals relate recombination hotspots can provide biological insights.

previous_headings: 5. Visualizing Linkage Signals Relative to Recombination Hotspots
title: Incorporating Recombination Rates into Polygenic Linkage Analysis
code: # Define hotspots as regions with rate > 2 cM/Mb rr_data <- copy(human_sexavg_RR_Bherer2017) rr_data[, is_hotspot := rate > 2.0]  # Summarize by chromosome hotspot_summary <- rr_data[, .(   total_length_Mb = (max(pos) - min(pos)) / 1e6,   avg_rate = mean(rate, na.rm = TRUE),   max_rate = max(rate, na.rm = TRUE),   hotspot_fraction = sum(is_hotspot, na.rm = TRUE) / .N ), by = chr]  print("Hotspot summary by chromosome:") print(hotspot_summary[chr %in% 1:5])

previous_headings: 5. Visualizing Linkage Signals Relative to Recombination Hotspots
what: 5.2 Comparing Gene Density to Recombination Rate
title: Incorporating Recombination Rates into Polygenic Linkage Analysis
code: # Analyze gene distribution relative to recombination # Using chr 1 as example chr1_genes <- Anatolia_EF_CLR[chr == 1] chr1_rr <- human_sexavg_RR_Bherer2017[chr == 1]  # Bin genes by position and compare to recombination rate bin_size <- 5e6  # 5 Mb bins chr1_genes[, bin := floor(startpos / bin_size) * bin_size] chr1_rr[, bin := floor(pos / bin_size) * bin_size]  gene_density <- chr1_genes[, .(n_genes = .N), by = bin] rr_by_bin <- chr1_rr[, .(avg_rate = mean(rate, na.rm = TRUE)), by = bin]  # Merge and plot combined <- merge(gene_density, rr_by_bin, by = "bin", all = TRUE) combined[is.na(n_genes), n_genes := 0] combined[is.na(avg_rate), avg_rate := 0]  par(mar = c(5, 4, 4, 4)) plot(combined$bin / 1e6, combined$n_genes, type = "h", col = "darkblue",      xlab = "Position (Mb)", ylab = "Gene Count", lwd = 2,      main = "Gene Density vs Recombination Rate (Chr 1)") par(new = TRUE) plot(combined$bin / 1e6, combined$avg_rate, type = "l", col = "red", lwd = 2,      xlab = "", ylab = "", axes = FALSE) axis(side = 4) mtext("Recombination Rate (cM/Mb)", side = 4, line = 2.5, col = "red") legend("topright", legend = c("Gene Density", "Recombination Rate"),        col = c("darkblue", "red"), lwd = 2, lty = c(1, 1))

previous_headings: 5. Visualizing Linkage Signals Relative to Recombination Hotspots
title: Incorporating Recombination Rates into Polygenic Linkage Analysis
text: interpreting pathway results, consider: Hotspot-enriched pathways: May show stronger signals due recombination-driven diversity Cold region pathways: LD may extend longer distances, requiring larger cgm.range Mixed regions: realistic; signals reflect true biological enrichment

previous_headings: 6. Differences Between Raw p-values and Recombination-Adjusted Results
title: Incorporating Recombination Rates into Polygenic Linkage Analysis

title: Polygenic Pathway Enrichment with Linkage Structure
text: PolyLinkR R package performs gene-based pathway enrichment, can also used evidence polygenic selection case software used selection signals evidence. package explicitly also accounts linkage disequilibrium adjacent loci belonging pathway. PolyLinkR introduces several improvements faster implementations popular polygenic selection tool PolySel, builds upon core file types summary statistics used PolySel. key difference PolyLinkR PolySel algorithm used generate null distribution pathway scores. PolySel performs standard permutation remap gene scores genes, whereas PolyLinkR uses permutation algorithm randomly links chromosomes/contigs single ‘circular’ genome, rotates circular genome create unique mapping genes gene scores. Importantly, randomisation process preserves innate linkage structure amongst genes across genome, limiting number potential false positives might arise otherwise. PolyLinkR uses four-step workflow: Read data plR_read() Generate permutation nulls plR_permute() Rescale autocorrelation plR_rescale() Prune identify significant sets plR_prune()

what: Gene set enrichment with control for confounding covariates — plR_permute
title: Gene set enrichment with control for confounding covariates — plR_permute
text: Performs gene set enrichment testing using gene-wise permutation procedure, accounting confounding variables. Note deconfounding step requires appropriately labeled covariate columns provided obj.info file (otherwise standard gene score residuals passed permutations). Applies Generalized Pareto Distribution (GPD) tail empirical null accurate estimation small p-values.

title: Gene set enrichment with control for confounding covariates — plR_permute
code: plR_permute(   plR.input,   permute = TRUE,   n.perm = 500000L,   n.boot = 30L,   alt = "upper",   md.meth = "robust",   kern.wt.max = 0.05,   kern.bound = "auto",   kern.func = "normal",   kern.scale = "auto",   gpd.cutoff = 5000L/n.perm,   seed = NULL,   verbose = TRUE,   n.cores = "auto",   fut.plan = "auto" )

title: Gene set enrichment with control for confounding covariates — plR_permute
text: plR.input plR class object; output polylinkR::plR_read. Required. permute logical; permutation step performed? Defaults TRUE. FALSE, gene score deconfounding performed without estimating set scores. TRUE, users must provide confounder covariates obj.info. useful exploring parameter settings impact deconfounded gene scores; permutation step can performed later passing output back plR_permute. n.perm integer; number permutations. Defaults 1e5. Must range [5e4L, Inf) exactly divisible 1e4. n.boot integer; number bootstrap replicates null inference. Defaults 30L. Must range [5, Inf). alt character; direction hypothesis test. Defaults "upper" enrichment upper tail (large set scores). Alternatively, "lower" tests enrichment lower tail (small values). "lower" chosen, data internally negated functions performing p-value estimation. md.meth character; determines whether raw covariate data ranks used Mahalanobis distance calculations. Defaults "robust", Mahalanobis distances converted ranks Spearman's metric used calculate covariance matrix. alternate option "raw" uses original covariates Pearson's covariance scaling. kern.wt.max numeric; maximum probability weight single gene. Defaults 0.05. Must range (1 / (n.genes - 1), 1]. Set 1 upper bound desired. Ignored covariate columns detected obj.info. kern.bound numeric; flanking region around gene weights overlapping genes inside region set 0. Weights partially overlapping genes downscaled proportion overlapping excluded region. Defaults 0.1 Mbp 0.1 cM (depending genetic coordinates). Range (0, Inf]; 0 denotes standard gene boundaries Inf excludes genes chromosome. Ignored appropriate covariate columns detected obj.info. kern.func character; kernel function used generate probability weights distances focal gene genes confounder space. Default "normal" (Gaussian kernel). Alternate options include "exponential" "inverse". Ignored covariate columns detected obj.info. kern.scale numeric; scalar used kernel function convert Mahalanobis distances probabilities. Defaults 2 Gaussian decay, log(10) exponential decay, 2 inverse decay. Range (0, Inf]. Ignored covariate columns detected obj.info. gpd.cutoff numeric; threshold tail probability apply GPD tail fitting. Defaults 500 / n.perm. Must range [max(c(1e-04, 500 / n.perm)), 0.05]. lower bound constraint ensures minimum 500 exceedances available GPD estimation, also ensuring compatibility empirical CDF, lowest evaluated quantile 1e-4. seed integer; random seed reproducibility. Preserved across subsequent polylinkR functions (plR_permute, plR_rescale). Defaults NULL, case seed generated automatically. Must within [-.Machine$integer.max, .Machine$integer.max]. verbose logical; progress messages printed console? Defaults TRUE. n.cores integer; number cores parallel processing. Defaults 1 maximum cores - 1. Must range [1, maximum cores]. fut.plan character; parallel backend future package. Defaults user n.cores choice checks available cores, choosing "sequential" single-core "multisession" multi-core systems. Options: "multisession", "multicore", "cluster", "sequential".

title: Gene set enrichment on scores rescaled for genetic autocorrelation — plR_rescale
text: plR.input plR class object; output polylinkR::plR_permute. Required. rescale logical; gene set score (.e.,setScore.std) rescaling performed? Defaults TRUE. FALSE, inter-gene autocorrelation estimated without revising enrichment testing rescaled (.e., decorrelated) gene set scores. useful exploring parameter settings impact gene set autocorrelation. rescaling step can performed later passing output plR_rescale, providing ac object ac argument. fast logical; gene set score rescaling performed using fast mode? Defaults TRUE, whereby analytical scaling factor \(1 / \sqrt{1 + (m - 1)\hat{\rho}}\) calculated covariance matrix ac used adjust empirical null GPD (scale threshold) parameters. FALSE, full empirical rescaling performed instead, recalculating score every permuted gene set. much computationally intensive intended users interested comparing analytical empirical results. ac data.frame data.table; user-provided object containing inter-gene autocorrelations. Include unique pairs genes non-zero autocorrelation. Genes must identified objIDs columns objID.objID.B. corresponding autocorrelation value must gamma. Defaults NULL. cgm.bin numeric; mimimum number gene pairs required bins empirical covariances. Default value = 30. Must range [10, 1e3]. Initially exponential grid bin sizes generated, favouring smaller bins short distances larger bins distant gene pairs. Bins genes successively merged larger bins minimum number genes reached. condition evaluated across chromosomes, ensuring bin smaller minimum value (final bins). cgm.range numeric; maximum inter-gene lag used evaluate autocovariance. Defaults 2e6 bp 2 cM, depending genetic distance measure. Must within interval [1e5, 5e7] bp [0.1, 50] cM. cgm.wt.max numeric; maximum probability weight single lag window. Defaults 0.1. Set 1 upper bound desired. Note lower bound reset 2 / . fitted lags chosen value falls limit. emp.bayes numeric; character string indicating empirical Bayes rescaling framework employed chromosome-level covariance beta coefficients. Users may choose fit either full reduced model, former model also allowing two coefficient correlated. default setting (auto) runs full model 15 chromosomes present, otherwise reduced model fit. cases full model assessed,  reduced model also fitted likelihood-based test used decide whether correlated random-effects structure supported. min.rho numeric; minimum estimated correlation two gene sets; values set 0. Defaults 1e-5. Range (0, 0.01]. verbose logical; progress reporting enabled? Defaults TRUE. n.cores integer; number cores parallel processing. Defaults 1 maximum cores - 1. Must [1, maximum cores]. fut.plan character; parallel backend future package. Defaults user n.cores choice checks cores, choosing "sequential" single-core "multisession" multi-core systems. Options: "multisession", "multicore", "cluster", "sequential".

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Assuming `my_plr` is the result of `polylinkR::plR_permute`  # Example 1: Basic usage new_plr <- plR_rescale(plR.input = my_plr)  # Example 2: Estimate autocorrelation only (no rescaling) new_plr <- plR_rescale(    plR.input = my_plr,    rescale = FALSE )  # Example 3: Custom variogram model and cores new_plr <- plR_rescale(   plR.input = my_plr,   vg.model  = c("Exp","Sph"),   n.cores   = 4 )  # Example 4: Using a user-provided autocovariance object # Assuming `my_ac` is a valid data.table or data.frame new_plr <- plR_rescale(    plR.input = my_plr,    ac        = my_ac )  # Or using the `new_plr` object generated by Example 2 new_plr <- plR_rescale(plR.input = my_rescaled_plr) } # }

title: polylinkR: Polygenic Pathway Enrichment with Linkage Structure — polylinkR-package
text: Perform gene-based pathway enrichment analysis accounting linkage disequilibrium (LD) amongst loci. Uses permutation approach preserves linkage structure generating null distributions pathway scores. reduces false positives compared standard permutation methods. version active development must used sole basis production clinical decisions without independent validation.
